I've got nothing against having `get_or_create()` method in the endpoint class.
___
However would you consider to raise custom error instead of `ValueError`? The reason is (as seems to me) that `.get_or_create()` you've implemented calls `.get()` which in it's turn invokes plenty different methods. At least `ValueError` can be raised from [this line](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/query.py#L43). I understand we can barely encounter on this cases, but anyway using custom error class can help to differentiate errors to avoid ambiguous.
___
Furthermore, there is `RequestError` in query.py module (at the same directory) which already has [logic to handle 404 NotFound error](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/query.py#L43) (at least to persist the context). But `RequestError` accepts a `Request` object in it's `__init__()`. And there is no such object in your `get_or_raise()` method, so you wouldn't raise one from .  
In my opinion original `get()` method should lift up the exception which would be handled by client code. IDK why originally there is [suppressing this exception](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/endpoint.py#L183) at all. This is similar to anti-pattern `try: ... except: pass` in fact.
___
My suggestion:
Just create `NotFoundError` at the same module and raise it instead of `ValueError`:
```
class NotFoundError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, name, **kwargs)
        self.message = f"{self.name} not found: {kwargs}"
    super().__init__(self.message)

...

class Endpoint:
    ...

    def get_or_raise(self, *args, **kwargs):
        r"""Queries the DetailsView of a given endpoint but raises an error if no object is returned
        :arg int,optional key: id for the item to be
            retrieved.
        :arg str,optional \**kwargs: Accepts the same keyword args as
            filter(). Any search argument the endpoint accepts can
            be added as a keyword arg.
        :returns: A single :py:class:`.Record` object
        :raises NotFoundError: if kwarg search returns zero or more than one item
        :Examples:
        Referencing with a kwarg that only returns one value.
        >>> nb.dcim.devices.get_or_raise(name='test1-a3-tor1b')
        test1-a3-tor1b
        >>>
        Referencing with an id.
        >>> nb.dcim.devices.get_or_raise(1)
        test1-edge1
        >>>
        """

        resp = self.get(*args, **kwargs)
        if resp is None:
            raise NotFoundError(f"{self.name} not found: {kwargs}")
        return resp

...
```

As for me, it'd be better to remove suppression from [this line](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/endpoint.py#L183), but it'd lead to that huge part of scripts using pynetbox face tracebacks with `RequestError` :)

So the bad news is that the accessibility is pretty bad.  Keyboard and assistive technology users are going to get confused quick.  Basically, you want to implement the [accordion pattern in the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/accordion/), but you're missing all the ARIA and necessary keyboard handlers.

The very good news is @bramkragten already put in some excellent a11y work creating the [`<ha-expansion-panel>`](https://github.com/home-assistant/frontend/blob/6f19ea1d8480a5ea6446bd09719fb6de26091ce3/src/components/ha-expansion-panel.ts) component, which implements the ARIA pattern nicely and has a nice big target button.  For a11y and just good UX consistency, can you switch it up to use that component?

```python
CompiledSQL(
    "SELECT addresses.user_id AS addresses_user_id, "
    "addresses.email_address AS addresses_email_address "
    # note the comma-separated FROM clause

    "AND addresses.id = anon_1.id ORDER BY addresses.id",

)

```diff

+++ test.py	2022-08-03 18:57:45.657305 +0000
@@ -1,8 +1,9 @@
 CompiledSQL(
-    "SELECT addresses.user_id AS addresses_user_id, "
-    "addresses.email_address AS addresses_email_address "
+    (
+        "SELECT addresses.user_id AS addresses_user_id, "

Ok so, after some investigation, the issue that you see in the tests occurs because you've edited the `_retry_status` (and `_retry_async_status` in a way that now no longer performs the `test_ping` call. 

However the tests expect this call to be performed, and we test the mocked socket for presence of certain data, corresponding to us sending a [Ping packet](https://wiki.vg/Protocol#Ping_Request), which now doesn't happen, hence the failure.

If you'll be removing this additional data, make sure to also remove the read data the mocked socket holds, representing the response to our Ping packet (being the [Pong packet](https://wiki.vg/Protocol#Ping_Response)).

Posted my comment at https://github.com/nipreps/fmriprep/pull/2894#issuecomment-1329308035, but meant to do it here:

> It doesn't seem to be new pip. Installing pip 22.1 in a Python 3.7 environment produces the same issue for me. I think there must be something in the dependency chain that's causing a resolution failure in 3.7. Pip's resolution mechanism is to find older and older versions of a tool, and we eventually found a nitime that is so old that it imports the entire module and breaks because numpy isn't a build dependency.
>
> I think the thing to do is to drop Python 3.7. [NEP29](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0029-deprecation_policy.html) recommended dropping it almost a year ago, and I assume that what we're seeing is the consequences of the broader ecosystem no longer testing on it. It's probably not worth a lot of effort tracking this down.
>
> I would suggest we take one of the following routes:
>
> 1) Adhere to NEP29 and drop Python minor releases along with the rest of the ecosystem.
> 2) Continue testing and advertise support for non-EOL Pythons until the fixes become non-obvious.

I'm currently thinking about how can keep our API correct and consistent in the long run. How do we justify adding the the `mirror` parameter only to some of our functions that use a `footprint` whose symmetry would impact their output. Rather than let a bug influence our API I would prefer to skip the perhaps a bit trivial `mirror` parameter and enable the user to do so beforehand with `mirror_footprint`.

Maybe we could add `mirror` temporarily to `binary_dilation` as part of a deprecation path? E.g.

- Release x: In `binary_dilation`, add `mirror=True`. Using `True` will raise a `FutureWarning` that mirroring will be removed in the future. Advise the user to use `mirror=False` and apply `mirror_footprint` if they need it.
- Release x+1: Remove the mirroring behavior. Using the `mirror` parameter at all raises a `FutureWarning` that the parameter is deprecated. 
- Release x+2: Remove the `mirror` parameter.

For users that use `footprint=None` anyway, we could reduce the noise by not raising deprecation warnings at all in that case. What do you think?

Your thoughtfulness is excellent.
I am using my free time to this PR because your proposal deals with how methods should be invoked with what args, that is also adjacent to static typing.

I am still concerned about the large number of `if` clauses in the `TestEntry`([This SO explains why avoiding such things](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27880631/why-should-you-avoid-conditional-logic-in-unit-tests-and-how)).
In particular, `mock_function` reproduces the behavior of `WINFUNCTYPE`(is my recognition right?).
But we need to ensure the correctness of this.

Rather, I prefer to create and inspect a mock that assumes the objects returned by the "raw" `WINFUNCTYPE`.
Following test code is hastily prepared, so it may be flawed in some ways, but please point them out to me if so.

```py
from comtypes import COMMETHOD
from ctypes import HRESULT

WSTRING = c_wchar_p
...

class Test_FixInOutArgs(ut.TestCase):
    def test_PortableDeviceApiLib_CreateObjectWithPropertiesAndData(self):
        spec = COMMETHOD(
            [],
            HRESULT,
            "CreateObjectWithPropertiesAndData",
            (["in"], POINTER(IUnknown), "pValues"),  # IPortableDeviceValues
            (["out"], POINTER(POINTER(IUnknown)), "ppData"),  # IStream
            (["in", "out"], POINTER(c_ulong), "pdwOptimalWriteBufferSize"),
            (["in", "out"], POINTER(WSTRING), "ppszCookie"),
        )
        m = MagicMock()
        fixed = _fix_inout_args(m, spec.argtypes, spec.paramflags)
        p_val = MagicMock(
            spec=POINTER(IUnknown), name="POINTER(IPortableDeviceValues)"
        )()
        pp_data = MagicMock(name="POINTER(POINTER(IStream))")()
        buf_size = MagicMock(spec=POINTER(c_ulong), name="POINTER(c_ulong)")()
        cookie = MagicMock(spec=POINTER(WSTRING), name="POINTER(c_wchar_p)")()
        m.return_value = (pp_data, ..., ...)
        ret_val = fixed(p_val, buf_size, cookie)
        self.assertEqual(ret_val, [pp_data, 1, None])
        m.assert_called_once()
        (m_1st, m_2nd, m_3rd), m_kw = m.call_args
        self.assertEqual(m_1st, p_val)
        self.assertEqual(m_2nd, buf_size)
        self.assertIsInstance(m_3rd, c_ulong)
        self.assertEqual(list(m_kw), ["ppszCookie"])  # ensure length and keys
        self.assertIsInstance(m_kw["ppszCookie"], c_wchar_p)
```
This PR deals with a difficult problem for test-driven development.
My code is only a suggestion, and there should be many different test perspectives.

Any opinions also would be appreciated.
Thank you.

I feel like this might become more readable by using f-strings (rather than `.format()`), by creating the message prior to raising it (to reduce the parenthetical levels), and by using `textwrap.dedent()` so the actual text can stay in-line with the code. Perhaps something like:

```python
message = textwrap.dedent(f"""\
    ...
    stdout:
    {textwrap.indent(result.stdout, " " * 6}
    ...
    """)
raise Exception(message)
```

This introduces sensitivity to the current indentation level (that `6` has to accommodate the current indentation level *of the code*, plus whatever indentation you actually want in the output), but may read more easily. What do you think about that approach?

Thanks for this contribution!

My main question is that we will now have two properties with a very similar content, which seems wasteful. We will never check these for validity.

Instead, my suggestion would be the following:

1. We only store the relative logdir path (let's call it `relative_logdir`)
2. `Trial.logdir` becomes a [cached property](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.cached_property) (use [this]{https://pypi.org/project/cached-property/) for python 3.7 compatibility, similar to dataclasses for 3.6)
3. For backwards compatibility, we should extract the relative path when `self.logdir` is set (`@property.setter`), but print a deprecation warning
4. Same for restoring pickled objects (if `state["logdir"]` is set, just call `self.logdir = state.pop("logdir")`

We should add (very short and simple) unit tests to show that these conversions work.

It would also be great to see a test where we move an experiment analysis directory and recover the same information, showing that relative path lookup works.

Let me know if you have bandwidth to add this, otherwise I'm happy to chime in!

A very cursory review that stopped short for another gut check. The warm cache speed of zip creation improving is nice to have - full stop. That said, it really does beg the question of why not just use `--layout packed` which has existed for quite some time and has the same speed benefits with the addition of friendlier network transfer characteristics / remote cache characteristics (which is why it was invented for Pants). You can rsync a packed PEX and win. You cannot do the same with the PEX built faster locally by this PR, but still being a totally new blob.

I feel comfortable with the correctness given the advisories about usage in the code.

I have a couple things that felt a little odd to me as I was reading, but none that make me want to block this from heading to `experimental` (but might be worth considering if we want to promote this out of experimental at some point):
- In thinking about what we're exposing on the `ScopeGraph`, I'm wondering if we want to lock down the public interface a bit more strictly as it seems possible to fairly easily break the representation by manipulating the public attributes (e.g., naively as a user I might think it safe to call `self.edges.add(foo)` w/o realizing that there is a private attribute that needs to track changes here)
- It's surprising that when I access `ScopeGraph.nodes` I'm not working with `ScopeTreeNodes` (I get that one is `ScopeTree` and one is `ScopeGraph` but we do build a `ScopeGraph` using `ScopeTreeNodes` so it all gets kind of 😵‍💫).

While I haven't thought about it _super_ deeply, I'm wondering if it might make sense to add something like a `ScopeGraph.add_scope()` method that will accept strings or `ScopeTreeNodes` and allow that to manage the properties of the graph so we don't need to leak some of these details, which I think would let us make `nodes` and `edges` private, and make `top_level_scopes` read-only...? But very possible I haven't thought through the downstream consequences sufficiently.

Regardless, this is non-blocking feedback IMO, so I am simultaneously hitting approve.

The addition of `close` is an interesting idea. Do you have a use case in mind? So far I've always used this for cases where there's some persistent data and subscribers are waiting for it to change, so "closing" isn't very intuitive -- the data might stop changing, but it's still there! skeptical about `coalesce=False`

Naming: naming is always hard, and especially for something like this, where there's no prior art (that I know of). But, it's important, since I think this is one of those cases where at first look the semantics are counterintuitive and hard to explain, even though they feel very natural once you wrap your head around them. So if the name can point people in the right direction, it'll help a lot.

"Stream" is maybe not ideal, because we use that as a term of art for byte streams. But the big thing I want is a name that hints at the coalescing, and the suitability for tracking persistent *state* rather than transient *events*.

`VersionTracker`? `UpdateNotifier`? `ChangeNotifier`? `Refresher`? `StateTracker`?
Personally would like if the names would be "7x3" / "5x4" instead of "3 lines" and "4 lines"

The main issue I see is in `_apply_norm`. If the perturbation has any `inf` or `-inf` values in it, the resultant vector will be all 0s and NaNs:
```
>>> grad
array([-inf, -10.,  -1.,   0.,   1.,  10.,  inf,   0.])
>>> grad / (np.sum(np.abs(grad), axis=ind, keepdims=True) + tol)
array([nan, -0., -0.,  0.,  0.,  0., nan,  0.])
>>> grad / (np.sqrt(np.sum(np.square(grad), axis=ind, keepdims=True)) + tol)
array([nan, -0., -0.,  0.,  0.,  0., nan,  0.])
```

Fixing this problem is difficult, as you need to define `inf / inf`, but at the least you should have a warning if infinite values appear in the gradient.

Since I personally never use `get_neighbors_by_distance`, I'm fine with any implementation. I just want to say that if the goal is to get a good number of neighbors, this algorithm is probably a bit too expensive, because it internally runs `_get_neighbors` possibly multiple times (which itself is a relatively expensive function). Besides, there's still no guarantee that you get the correct number of neighbors in the end, since `_get_neighbors` itself relies on `_get_boundary_layer_width`, which estimates the width required from the density. In total, what in my opinion makes the most sense is to estimate the number of atoms from the global density (or Voronoi, if you really want to be sure), i.e. it should be:

```
num_neighbors = (1+buffer)*length**3/volume_per_atom
```

And maybe it's up to the user to determine `buffer`.

I appreciate the effort but I'd recommend consulting on the design and desirability of such an impactful change.

In the issue, you wrote
> I need to get RawHeaders from BodyPartReader and from MultipartReader

But as maintainers, we must consider what an average user needs, not one in a million. Any change like this will affect all the users and, of course, we wouldn't want such a penalty on them.

Moreover, it's best to explain your motivation with something more than "I want this" because the solution you invented may be architecturally wrong and nobody would be able to judge whether what you're proposing is even the right thing to do. Start with what you're attempting to solve with all the details and constraints, and code snippets. And only then follow up with how you envision that, including an explanation of why you think this proposal is good. It's also recommended to think about whether anybody besides you would benefit from this and whether it would be harmful in some cases. Of course, we'll help with the latter during the discussion/reviews but it's always preferable to learn such things before investing much time/effort into the implementation.

> I need to generate ICAP Response. ICAP packet consist of HTTP request or (and) HTTP response.

aiohttp is an HTTP server (and a client for that matter). Supporting other protocols is usually out of scope. And since we care a lot about the performance and memory consumption penalties, this is something we take into account when deciding whether the proposal is worthy.

I've marked some problematic changes in the comments below but besides that, I'm going to leave this for @asvetlov to accept or reject.

P.S. When filing issues and PR descriptions, it's a good tone to actually fill them out. Otherwise, you're forcing the reviewers to re-ask those same questions, increasing the latency of getting to the final decision.

Will review the code, but first two quick design questions - cc @jtamiace 

## Icon buttons

It's rare in Kolibri (versus Studio) to use purely icon buttons rather than text-based so I wanted to make sure this was intentional. This is partly because learners on tablets don't have tooltips, and partly because learners are less likely to know iconography conventions in contrast to Studio where we use icon buttons more liberally for "power users".

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134698606-066257ee-2b83-48f4-bd11-3f3f8a979e59.png)

For example, even in coach UIs we spell out the word 'remove':

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134702357-551ca56c-7562-4072-8257-526335cd3bd9.png)


If we do stick with icon buttons, note that we might be using the wrong one for 'delete':

* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-clear
* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-delete

## Empty states

We've made 'empty states' more consistent, and I think this design deviates slightly from previous directions:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/5051
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/3550

I would have expected it to look more like:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134700995-1dd393a3-9368-4a96-a45e-e516bbf0ea02.png)

Perhaps something to track in KDS?

Hi @qzchenwl! Thanks for the PR. A few concerns:

  - Adding special casing in our code for JupyterHub is a large
    maintenance burden for us. Most obviously, it means that we need to
    know what it is an how to test changes on it. Our build, test, and
    deploy processes don’t have any baggage related to Kubernetes or
    Docker or The Cloud, and changing that would be expensive.

  - The existing system passes args from `%tensorboard ARGS...` directly
    down to the TensorBoard binary. In particular, we do not attempt to
    parse, inspect, or modify the arguments, and this is by design.
    Correctly parsing flags would require discovering and executing
    arbitrary plugin code in the host IPython environment, would
    introduce an interdependency between the host `%tensorboard` and the
    target binary, etc.

better on these two constraints?

single character; `/` is a common choice, but if there are lots of

way, you don’t need to escape the slashes, and it’s easier to see which
parts of the expression are which.

You requested help on what to do for the audit logs documentation so.

1. Every new key that's added in the `changes` dict is documented under `AuditLogDiff`
2. Every new action type is added to `AuditLogAction`
3. The action type mentions what the type of `target` is and what is expected.
4. Those new action types document every possible `AuditLogDiff` value you can receive from it to the best of your ability.
5. If that action type has a special `extras` then it gets documented there.

There are a lot of pre-existing examples you can just copy paste and adapt from.

The results on the jupyter notebooks are impressive. It will be a great addition for the community to have support for the arbor simulator. It is also worthy to show that BluePyOpt does not have to depend on a single simulator. While looking forward to seeing this important change merged, I have some remarks on the implementation.

1)
In the `create_acc.py` there is a lot of `list of dict of dict of list of list` types of data structures to store arbor mechanisms, section_mechs, global_mechs, pprocess_mechs, global_scaled_mechs, section_scaled_mechs, ranges, template names, global properties, local properties, channels and loc (location) values.

The problem with using dicts of dicts (instead of objects instantiated from python classes) in the system design is that they are obscuring the structure and forcing you to read the entire code to understand the structure. 
For example, I cannot tell if an arbor catalogue consists of mechanisms or if an arbor mechanisms consists of catalogues. Which one contains the other? Where does the loc (location) stand in the hierarchy? What is the relationship between those and channels and templates? Are ranges and globals both on the same hierarchy of nestedness? I need understand those relationships without having to read the whole code. Also it (dict of dict of list of *) makes it very hard to introduce a new change in the code.  

At least the top level of the hierarchy should be visible. Here for example one can understand the relationship between a Catalogue and a Mechanisms and pprocess_mechs without reading the implementation. One can easily make changes on the structure. Modern IDE and static code analysis tools can detect possible bugs before runtime.

```python
class Mechanisms:
    def __init__(self, global_mechs, section_mechs, pprocess_mechs) -> None:
        self.global_mechs = global_mechs
        self.section_mechs = section_mechs
        self.pprocess_mechs = pprocess_mechs


class Catalogue:
    def __init__(self, name, mechanisms) -> None:
        self.name = name
        self.mechanisms = mechanisms

```





2)  I left 16 other remarks on the review.

My main concern is this can cause some regression by overloaded raylet. Ideally, we should also do backpressure from the raylet side to limit max # of concurrent lease requests, but I don't think we have time... 

I wonder if we can just do some CPU usage benchmarking and see how many lease requests ray can handle before being overloaded? I think we can do something like this ;

1. Set lease request very large (10 -> 500 -> 1K -> 10K -> 50K -> 100K for example)
2. Submit many tasks from a head node (maybe sth like 100K). 
3. Compare CPU usage of raylets among tests.

Another question is fairness. When we have 3 categories (A, B, C), 2 task each, do we submit tasks in 

1. A1, B1, C1, A2, B2, C2
2. or A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, C2
?

I think if it is 2, it can also has unexpected regression. I am just curious what's the current policy

Sorry for the very long delay! I wanted to be sure to test this, and it took me a while to set up a test environment. We appreciate your patience.

Now that I've done that, I'm sorry to say that unless you provide some information that reveals that I made a mistake in testing,  **I don't support the changes in this PR**.

@Arka161 how exactly did you test these changes? Please be as specific as possible.

It's strange to me that image-building is failing for you on `master` (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/5369#issuecomment-1205738517) and succeeding on this PR, when I see the opposite. It's also surprising to me that the changes proposed in this PR are working for you.

* I'd expect that any patching of keys needs to happen *before* `apt-get update` and `apt-get install`, but you've placed it after those commands.
* with only the changes in this PR, I don't understand how the use of `conda activate` could present an error for me but not for you

Maybe the difference between how you tested this change and how I tested it (described below) will reveal what I'm missing.

### How I tested

I got a GPU-enabled EC2 instance on AWS with the following specifications.

- **AMI**: `Ubuntu 18.04 (ami-0b152cfd354c4c7a4)`
- **instance type**: `g4dn.xlarge` (1 NVIDIA T4 GPU)
- **region**: `us-west-2`
- **root volume size**: 100GB

Set up docker and CUDA drivers on it.

<details><summary>CUDA setup (click me)</summary>

```shell
# following https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/install-guide.html

# install docker
sudo apt-get update -y
sudo apt-get install -y \
    ca-certificates \
    curl \
    gnupg \
    lsb-release

curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg \
| sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg

echo \
  "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \
  $(lsb_release -cs) stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

sudo apt-get update -y
sudo apt-get install -y \
    docker-ce \
    docker-ce-cli \
    containerd.io

sudo systemctl enable docker
sudo systemctl start docker

# install nvidia-docker
distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID) \
      && curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg \
      && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/$distribution/libnvidia-container.list | \
            sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \
            sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID) \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo apt-key add - \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/$distribution/libnvidia-container.list | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID) \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/nvidia-docker/gpgkey | sudo apt-key add - \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/nvidia-docker/$distribution/nvidia-docker.list | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

# get nvidia drivers and tools
# (versions selected by choosing the newest thing shown in `apt search nvidia-driver`)
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:graphics-drivers/ppa --yes
sudo apt-get update -y
sudo apt-get install -y \
    nvidia-driver-515 \
    nvidia-utils-515

# reboot the machine
sudo reboot
```

Next, created a small Python test script that trains a binary classification model using LightGBM's GPU support.

<details><summary>test-training.py (click me)</summary>

```shell
mkdir -p "${HOME}/repos/pr-5369"
cd "${HOME}/repos/pr-5369"

cat << EOF > ./test-training.py
import lightgbm as lgb
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)

dtrain = lgb.Dataset(
    data=X,
    label=y
)

model = lgb.train(
    params={
        "objective": "binary",
        "num_iterations": 10,
        "verbose": 1,
        "num_leaves": 8,
        "device": "gpu"
    },
    train_set=dtrain,
)
print("--- done training ---")
EOF
```

Thanks! This generally looks good. I commented with a few fixes that I found by running our existing test suite and a suggestion for how to extend this further to allow varying these credentials per code location.

https://docs.dagster.io/community/contributing includes directions for getting your environment set up for testing. But because this is a narrowly scoped change, you might find that setup to be a bit heavy handed. Instead, I think the following should be sufficient to run the relevant tests:

```
pip install 'tox==3.25.0'
python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/
tox -- -k ecs_tests
```

These tests run against a local mock of ECS. That mock uses [botocore's stubber](https://botocore.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/stubber.html) to ensure that calls to the actual ECS API are valid.

Historically, when we've extended our ECS code in similar ways ([like when we added volumes and mount points](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/13409/files#diff-83428dfde55d54583a7e6470d4f59378558e53d2ddce1143f98d02f7f7fd0b67)), we've also extended the existing tests to pass in the new parameter and assert that it gets set as expected.

We don't run any integration tests on this particular code but I'd recommend once you feel like your changes are sufficient, you run it on one of your own deployments and confirm that you can pull from your third-party repository.
